pathophysiology_summary: This patient has known primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) and has been unable to absorb her regular oral hydrocortisone and fludrocortisone due to a vomiting illness over the past 24 hours. Without adequate cortisol, the body cannot mount an adequate stress response or maintain vascular tone and blood pressure, precipitating an acute adrenal (Addisonian) crisis. The combination of refractory hypotension, profound weakness, altered mental status, and an intercurrent illness in a known Addisonian patient is diagnostic. This is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate parenteral hydrocortisone and IV fluid resuscitation; delay significantly increases mortality risk. Unlike most emergencies, patients with Addison's disease are often issued an emergency injectable hydrocortisone kit specifically for this scenario, and prompt bystander/self-administration while awaiting the ambulance is an evidence-based, guideline-endorsed bridging action.

differential.evidence_basis: Known Addison's disease with inability to absorb oral steroids during a vomiting illness, plus severe weakness and near-syncope.
Vomiting illness could cause dehydration and postural dizziness alone; must be distinguished from crisis given the known adrenal insufficiency.

differential_progression.scoring_note: In any known Addisonian patient with vomiting and inability to take oral steroids, adrenal crisis must be presumed and treated as such until proven otherwise.

differential.evidence_basis: Confusion (DI-003) and near-syncope confirm haemodynamic compromise and CNS effects consistent with crisis, not simple gastroenteritis.
Confusion and severe hypotension in a known Addisonian patient unable to take steroids strongly favour crisis over simple dehydration.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage the case should be treated as an unequivocal emergency requiring immediate ambulance dispatch.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis requiring immediate parenteral hydrocortisone and emergency transport.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Any plan without immediate 000 dispatch and either bystander-administered emergency hydrocortisone (if available) or advice to proceed straight to ED is inadequate.

red_flags.description: Inability to take/retain oral steroid replacement during an intercurrent illness in a known Addisonian patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Precipitates acute cortisol deficiency and crisis; this alone is an indication for emergency steroid dosing.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to refractory shock and death without emergency steroid replacement.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Reflects haemodynamic compromise from cortisol/mineralocorticoid deficiency.

red_flags.description: Altered mental status/confusion reported by spouse
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates significant physiological compromise (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia) requiring immediate emergency care.

red_flags.description: Refractory hypotension (85/50) and buccal hyperpigmentation (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms haemodynamic crisis and chronic ACTH excess (hyperpigmentation) typical of primary adrenal insufficiency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor via chat.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: AI Doctor may attribute all symptoms to a simple vomiting bug and suggest oral rehydration, missing that the real emergency is the inability to absorb life-sustaining steroid replacement in a known Addisonian patient.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 ambulance dispatch; advise administration of the patient's own previously-prescribed emergency injectable hydrocortisone while awaiting the ambulance if available
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Any delay in recognising this as crisis rather than simple gastroenteritis risks fatal deterioration.
